author: Dr. Sarah Beckman / Dr. David Park, PGY-1
content:
ADMISSION HISTORY AND PHYSICAL — JOHNS HOPKINS HOSPITAL
Date/Time: 3/15/2027, 4:30 PM
Patient: Sandoval, Maria Isabel (MRN: MRN-90215734)
DOB: 8/12/2003 (Age 23)
Admission source: Emergency Department (presented at 12:45 PM)
Admitting team: General Medicine (Dr. Beckman attending, Dr. Castro PGY-3, Dr. Park PGY-1)
Interpreter: Spanish (Language Line); mother and aunt at bedside.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
'No me puedo mover, no me puedo comer' — 'I can't move, I can't eat.'

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Sandoval is a 23-year-old Spanish-speaking woman with active systemic lupus erythematosus (with new inflammatory myositis and oropharyngeal dysphagia per February admission), Hashimoto's thyroiditis, severe protein-calorie malnutrition, and a long history of fragmented care due to absence of health insurance, who presents from her home directly to the ED via her mother and aunt (brought in by family vehicle, not EMS) with progressive multi-system deterioration over the 5 weeks since her AMA discharge on 2/11/2027.

Course since AMA discharge (per available records, family report, and Esperanza Health phone notes):

- 2/15/2027: Hopkins rheumatology follow-up (Dr. Tanaka, financial-aid grant). Patient continued on prednisone 30 mg, HCQ 200 mg BID, MMF 1 g BID, levothyroxine. Weight 41.6 kg. CK 1490, ESR 58, anti-dsDNA 36, albumin 2.4. Skin biopsy preliminary read returned 2/12: 'interface dermatitis with vacuolar changes at the dermal-epidermal junction; superficial perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate; areas of pallor of the upper epidermis with focal flattening' — interpreted by dermatology as compatible with SLE-related interface dermatitis BUT WITH FEATURES THAT COULD ALSO BE SEEN IN NUTRITIONAL DERMATOSIS (kwashiorkor-associated). Recommended clinical correlation and consideration of nutritional intervention.

- 2/22/2027: SCHEDULED EMG and muscle biopsy were NOT performed. Family report: morning of the appointment, patient was too weak to leave the apartment. They could not get her down the 3 flights of stairs. Mother called to reschedule; appointment moved to 3/8/2027. Patient was not seen this date.

- Late February — early March: Progressive worsening at home:
  * Dysphagia: now profound. Mother reports patient can no longer manage pureed solids without choking. She has been on liquids and small amounts of Ensure for ~10 days. Last solid food was ~3/3/2027 (a small piece of soft bread that she choked on; family afraid to feed her solids since). Mother estimates intake of ~400-600 kcal/day, mostly Ensure and small sips of broth.
  * Weakness: profound. Patient is now bedbound. Unable to rise from bed without 2-person assistance. Cannot lift arms to brush her hair. Speaks softly with nasal quality. Voice now described as 'whispery.'
  * Weight: family has not had access to scale; patient looks emaciated to mother.
  * Skin: scaling has WORSENED markedly. Now diffuse pinkish-brown scaling over elbows, knees, shins, dorsum of hands, AND has spread to the trunk in patches. Mother describes peeling, flaking skin in some areas. Hair has continued to thin and now appears very brittle, with patches of lighter color at the base of strands ('flag sign' visible on inspection).
  * Edema: significant. Mother says patient's face is 'puffy' all day now, not just in the mornings; ankles swollen; abdomen looks 'bloated.'
  * Bowel/bladder: bowel movements infrequent (every 3-4 days; small, formed). Urinating less than before but no dysuria.
  * Mental status: family describes patient as 'sad and very tired'; she 'sleeps most of the day.' Not confused. Responds when spoken to.

- 3/8/2027: RESCHEDULED EMG/muscle biopsy NOT performed. Mother attempted to bring patient via Lyft; patient was too weak to transfer to the vehicle. Hopkins rheumatology nursing line spoken to that day; recommended ED evaluation. Mother and aunt did not want to go to ED that day because patient 'was OK at home' and could swallow Ensure.

- 3/10/2027: Esperanza Health PCP (Dr. Williams) was called by mother; he urgently recommended ED evaluation, but mother said she could not take her at that time.

- 3/12-3/14/2027 (past 3 days): Patient stopped most oral intake. Drinking only small sips of water. Mother reports patient slept >20 hours yesterday. Today she felt patient's arms 'felt cold' and she was 'breathing strange' — somewhat shallow and faster than usual. Mother panicked and brought her in.

Review of systems otherwise denied: fever, cough, chest pain, hemoptysis, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, abdominal pain, dysuria, gross hematuria, headache, focal weakness on one side, vision changes, seizures.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Systemic lupus erythematosus with inflammatory myositis (per 2/8/2027 admission workup)
- Hashimoto's thyroiditis
- Severe protein-calorie malnutrition (confirmed 2/8/2027 admission, presumed worsened)
- Iron-deficiency anemia (2018, resolved)
- Latent TB ruled out (2018)
- Discharged AMA 2/11/2027 from this hospital

MEDICATIONS (verified with family):
- Prednisone 30 mg PO daily (taking; runs out tomorrow, 3/16/2027)
- Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO BID (taking)
- Mycophenolate mofetil 1 g PO BID (ran out 3/9/2027 — no further supply secured)
- Levothyroxine 25 mcg PO daily (taking inconsistently — "two or three times a week" per mother)
- TMP/SMX 80/400 mg PO M/W/F for PCP prophylaxis (taking when she remembers, last dose ~3/10)
- Thiamine 100 mg PO daily (ran out late Feb)
- Calcium 500 mg BID, Vitamin D 1000 IU daily (taking inconsistently)
- Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (taking)

ALLERGIES: NKDA.

FAMILY HISTORY: Maternal aunt with SLE; mother with hypothyroidism, pre-diabetes; father (deceased) with treated TB.

SOCIAL HISTORY: Lives with mother, younger brother, maternal aunt in East Baltimore. Not working since November 2026. Insurance: emergency Medicaid coverage active for this admission (will be evaluated for ongoing Medicaid after discharge). Speaks Spanish primarily; understands limited English. Denies tobacco, alcohol, drug use. Has not had sex in over 1 year. No recent travel. Pets: none.

VITALS ON ARRIVAL (12:45 PM, ED):
BP 86/52, HR 124, RR 26, T 97.2 F (36.2 C — mildly hypothermic), SpO2 94% on RA -> 97% on 2L NC. Weight 35.4 kg (CRITICAL DROP — down 5.8 kg from 2/11/2027 AMA discharge weight of 41.2 kg; total loss from 1/12/2026 baseline of 50.8 kg = 15.4 kg / 30.3%). Height 150 cm. BMI 15.7 (SEVERE underweight per WHO). Pain 4/10 (diffuse soreness, weakness > pain).

Updated vitals on arrival to floor (4:15 PM, after 2L NS bolus held due to edema concerns; 1L given over 4 hours):
BP 96/58, HR 110, RR 22, T 97.6 F, SpO2 96% on 2L NC.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: SEVERELY CACHECTIC young woman, age-appropriate cognition, mother holding her hand. Visibly emaciated with prominent temporal wasting, sunken cheeks, prominent clavicles. Bilateral lower-extremity and facial edema present despite cachexia (anasarca-like presentation). Skin pale; nail beds pale. Patient is alert and oriented x 3 but speaks in soft, nasal, somewhat slow Spanish.

HEAD/EYES: Temporal wasting bilaterally. Conjunctivae very pale. Sclerae anicteric. PERRL. EOM intact but slight bilateral ptosis noted (could be asthenic ptosis vs ocular involvement of myositis vs simple fatigue). Mild bilateral periorbital edema.

ORAL/PHARYNX: Lips somewhat cracked and dry but moist on inspection. Several small (~2-3 mm) shallow ulcers on hard palate (3) and tongue (1). Oropharynx without exudate. Voice nasal, somewhat hoarse. No drooling at rest but patient does not handle her oral secretions perfectly — small amount of pooled saliva noted during exam. Soft palate elevation reduced bilaterally with mild asymmetry on left.

NECK: Trachea midline. No lymphadenopathy. Thyroid not enlarged. JVP not elevated but difficult to assess given edema.

CARDIOVASCULAR: Tachycardic 110-124, regular rhythm. No murmur audible. Distant heart sounds. Capillary refill 4 seconds at the fingertips. Pulses thready but symmetric.

PULMONARY: RR 22-26, shallow respirations. Decreased breath sounds at bilateral bases, no crackles, no wheezing. No tactile fremitus changes. Cough is weak — patient cannot clear strong cough on command.

ABDOMEN: Distended (anasarca with abdominal wall edema). Soft, non-tender to palpation. Bowel sounds quiet but present. No organomegaly appreciable. Mild pretibial pitting +2 edema; sacral +1 edema.

SKIN: REMARKABLE FINDINGS.
- Diffuse scaling, flaking, pinkish-brown dermatosis covering the trunk (chest, abdomen, back), extensor surfaces of upper and lower extremities, and dorsum of hands. In some areas there is overlying flaking/desquamation. Skin has a 'crackled' or 'flaky paint' appearance in some areas of the abdomen and thighs — described historically with kwashiorkor.
- Skin overall thin, dry, with reduced turgor.
- Hair: diffuse scalp thinning. On inspection, hair appears brittle and there are bands of color change at the proximal hair shafts — 'flag sign' (alternating bands of normal pigmentation and lighter pigmentation along single hair strands, consistent with episodes of nutritional insufficiency).
- Nails: pale, brittle, with mild transverse depressions on several fingernails (Beau lines suggesting a stressful event).
- No discoid lesions. No purpura. No vasculitic ulcers.
- Skin biopsy site (left elbow, from 2/10/2027) healed but with surrounding scaling.

MUSCULOSKELETAL:
- Joint exam: no overt synovitis appreciated today (likely masked by edema and steroids); mild tenderness at MCPs only. Joints with reduced range due to edema and weakness more than pain.
- Muscle bulk: visibly reduced; deltoid, quadriceps, gluteal muscle wasting prominent.
- Strength testing (limited by patient effort/fatigue):
  * Hip flexion: 2/5 bilaterally (cannot lift leg off bed against gravity; can slide)
  * Knee extension: 3/5 bilaterally
  * Hip extension: 2/5
  * Shoulder abduction: 2/5 bilaterally
  * Elbow flexion: 3+/5
  * Wrist extension: 4/5
  * Grip strength: 3/5
  * Neck flexion: 2/5 (head lag with attempted sit-up)
  * Distal extremities: 4-5/5 throughout
- Cannot stand from bed without 2-person assist.
- Reflexes 1+ throughout, symmetric.

NEUROLOGIC:
Mental status: Alert, oriented x 3 (person, place, today's date). Engages with interpreter. Affect somewhat flat but appropriate. Speech: dysarthric (nasal, weak). Cranial nerves: CN II - PERRLA, fundi not well visualized; CN III/IV/VI - EOM intact, slight bilateral asthenic ptosis; CN V - facial sensation intact; CN VII - face symmetric at rest, but smile and forehead movements weaker bilaterally; CN VIII - hearing grossly intact; CN IX/X - palate elevation reduced and slightly asymmetric, gag reflex sluggish; CN XI - shoulder shrug 3+/5; CN XII - tongue protrudes midline with reduced excursion. Sensation: intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration symmetrically. Coordination: cannot perform finger-to-nose smoothly due to weakness more than ataxia. Gait: cannot ambulate; bedbound currently.

ED COURSE TODAY:
12:45 PM: Patient triaged ESI 2. Immediately moved to a critical care bay.
13:00: IV access x 2 obtained. Initial labs drawn (see LAB012). Cardiac monitor. Pulse oximetry. Aspiration precautions; HOB up 30 degrees; suction at bedside.
13:15: Initial labs return — pancytopenia, severe hypoalbuminemia (1.6), severe hypoproteinemia, hyponatremia (130), hypokalemia (3.0), hypocalcemia (7.8), hypomagnesemia (1.4), hypophosphatemia (1.8), elevated AST/ALT, CK 2360, troponin negative, lactate 2.1.
13:30: Cautious 500 mL bolus NS started (rate-limited given edema and refeeding concerns). NPO confirmed.
13:45: Hopkins rheumatology (Dr. Park, fellow) and nutrition (Ms. Eun-Ji Lee, RD) paged. Multi-team huddle initiated.
14:00: Patient discussed with attending and accepted to general medicine with intent for likely ICU upgrade if she does not stabilize. Family informed.
14:00-15:00: CXR (clear, low-volume), CT chest/abd/pelvis (see IMG004 — preliminary read: no malignancy, no acute findings; modest small-volume ascites; cachectic body habitus; mild bilateral pleural effusions; trace pericardial effusion). EKG: sinus tachycardia 118; no acute changes; normal intervals.
15:30: NPO maintained. Thiamine 200 mg IV given BEFORE any dextrose or carbohydrate. Multivitamin IV given. Cautious electrolyte repletion initiated: K 20 mEq PO held (NPO), 10 mEq IV over 2 hours; Mg 2 g IV over 2 hours; Phos repletion plan deferred until repeat (will not over-correct given refeeding risk).
16:00: Transferred to medicine floor with telemetry. ICU bed not deemed necessary at this time.
16:30: This admission H&P documented at bedside in real time with patient, mother, and aunt present.

INPATIENT WORKING DIAGNOSIS (PROVISIONAL):

Primary (presumed): ACTIVE SLE with INFLAMMATORY MYOSITIS, with significant OROPHARYNGEAL DYSPHAGIA causing severe protein-calorie starvation and ANASARCA / KWASHIORKOR-LIKE SYNDROME.

Unifying clinical-causal hypothesis:
SLE -> inflammatory myositis affecting pharyngeal muscles -> progressive dysphagia -> profound food intake limitation amplified by social-determinant barriers (insurance loss, food insecurity, language barrier, intermittent immunosuppression) -> severe protein-calorie malnutrition -> hypoalbuminemia -> anasarca -> dermatosis with features of kwashiorkor.

Key supporting findings: CK 2360 + proximal weakness pattern + nasal voice + dysphagia (myositis); albumin 1.6 + anasarca + scaling dermatosis with 'flaky paint' appearance + 'flag sign' hair (kwashiorkor); rapid weight loss 30.3% from baseline; clear historical timeline showing dysphagia emergence -> intake decline -> body composition collapse.

Key differential considerations to actively rule in / out:
- Dermatomyositis (overlapping with SLE): Gottron-like changes possible; need biopsy direction; MSA panel including anti-MDA5, anti-TIF1, anti-Mi-2 pending.
- HIV / opportunistic infection: prior screens negative; will recheck; CMV, EBV studies pending.
- Disseminated TB: TB-IGRA was negative 2/9/2027; given immunosuppression and recent decline, repeat IGRA + induce sputum for AFB.
- Lymphoma / malignancy: CT chest/abd/pelvis negative for adenopathy or mass (reassuring); peripheral smear pending.
- Inflammatory bowel disease with malabsorption: presentation atypical, but to be considered with stool studies and ESR/CRP context.
- Drug-induced myositis: HCQ rare, MMF rare; pause MMF given clinical picture and ongoing dx workup.
- Hyperthyroid myopathy: TSH was elevated (subclinically); free T4 and T3 to clarify; less likely.
- Sarcoidosis: less likely; will be evaluated by ID/rheum.
- Selenium deficiency cardiomyopathy / Keshan-like picture: low yield but selenium level being ordered given nutritional state.

PROBLEM LIST AT ADMISSION:

1. Acute decompensation of multi-system illness — anasarca with severe hypoalbuminemia (1.6), shock physiology mild (HR 124, BP 86/52 initially, mild hypothermia).

2. Inflammatory myositis (presumed SLE-related vs DM-overlap): CK 2360, aldolase 28, proximal weakness 2-3/5, dysphagia, dysphonia. Workup: EMG, muscle biopsy, myositis-specific antibody panel.

3. Severe oropharyngeal dysphagia with aspiration risk: cannot manage solids; only thickened liquids ok per Feb MBSS; will likely need NG tube or PEG; SLP repeat MBSS planned.

4. Severe protein-calorie malnutrition / KWASHIORKOR-PATTERN: albumin 1.6, prealbumin 6 (severely low), anasarca, scaling dermatosis with 'flaky paint' areas, 'flag sign' hair, Beau's lines. Nutrition with high refeeding risk.

5. Active SLE: anti-dsDNA 48 (now markedly elevated), C3 58 / C4 5 (markedly low), mild proteinuria, 4 oral ulcers, cytopenias.

6. Pancytopenia: Hgb 7.8, WBC 2.30, Plt 122; ANC 1.0; potentially multifactorial (SLE, nutrition, MMF effect, marrow suppression).

7. Multiple electrolyte derangements: Na 130, K 3.0, Mg 1.4, Ca 7.8, Phos 1.8 — HIGH REFEEDING RISK.

8. Hepatocellular pattern enzyme elevation: AST 142, ALT 86 — likely myositis with some hepatic component; will follow.

9. Mild renal: UPC 0.58, possibly mildly worsening; creatinine 0.4 (likely underestimate given low muscle mass — true GFR may be considerably lower than calculated).

10. Hashimoto's thyroiditis with intermittent adherence: TSH 6.84 today.

11. Risk of opportunistic infection on immunosuppression with severe malnutrition: PCP prophylaxis with TMP/SMX (intermittent); CMV/EBV PCR pending; consider antifungal screen.

12. Severe social-determinant barriers: insurance fragmentation, food insecurity, language barrier, transportation barrier. Emergency Medicaid active during this admission. Care management, social work, and Spanish-speaking patient advocate engaged from admission.

ADMISSION PLAN:

General measures:
- NPO until repeat MBSS (scheduled for hospital day 2-3).
- HOB up 30 degrees at all times; aspiration precautions.
- Telemetry; daily ECG.
- Strict input/output, daily weights, ortho vitals.
- DVT prophylaxis with mechanical compression devices ONLY (no chemoprophylaxis until platelets verified and bleeding risk reviewed).
- Pressure ulcer prevention; turn q2h.

Nutrition (high refeeding risk — slow approach):
- Day 1 (today): NPO. IV fluids 0.9% NS at 50 mL/hr only (mindful of edema). Thiamine 200 mg IV daily x 5 days (already started). Multivitamin IV daily. Folic acid 1 mg IV daily.
- Day 2: Place small-bore NG tube for enteral feeding after confirming swallow study scheduled and starting feeding plan. Start at 10 kcal/kg/day = ~360 kcal/day initially (slow ramp).
- Plan to advance 5 kcal/kg/day every 24 hours if electrolytes (K, Mg, Phos) tolerate.
- Replete electrolytes BEFORE each escalation.
- Goal eventual: 25-30 kcal/kg/day = ~1000-1200 kcal/day for maintenance; will need higher for repletion eventually.
- Hold all caloric IV fluids (no dextrose, no parenteral nutrition initially).

Rheumatology / immunosuppression:
- Hold MMF (already off since 3/9). Will reintroduce after biopsy direction and infection screen clear.
- Continue prednisone 30 mg PO daily for now (no escalation until biopsy + infection screen clarity).
- Hold consideration of pulse steroids until pre-immunosuppression infection screen clean.
- Continue HCQ 200 mg BID.
- Pre-immunosuppression intensification infection screen (drawn today): TB-IGRA repeat, sputum AFB x3 (need induction given weak cough), HBV/HCV/HIV repeat, CMV PCR, EBV PCR, beta-glucan, galactomannan, strongyloides serology.
- Skin biopsy: repeat from a different site — request dermatology consult.
- EMG and muscle biopsy: needed urgently this admission; consult neurology + IR.
- Modified barium swallow study: SLP consult; repeat MBSS hospital day 2-3.
- Echo and pulmonary function tests (PFTs): SLE workup including possible early cardiopulmonary involvement.
- Renal: 24-hour urine for protein and creatinine clearance; consider renal biopsy if proteinuria escalates.

ID:
- Continue TMP/SMX 80/400 mg M/W/F (PCP prophylaxis) — ensure patient receives consistently.
- Add fluconazole if culture data or clinical suggest invasive candidiasis.
- Reassess varicella zoster prophylaxis.

Electrolytes:
- IV K 10 mEq over 2 hours (already given); recheck. Plan to replete to 4.0 once Mg corrected.
- IV Mg 2 g over 2 hours (already given); recheck.
- Phos: cautious IV repletion at 0.16 mmol/kg over 6 hours given level 1.8; recheck q6h.
- Avoid aggressive bicarbonate; mild acidosis acceptable.

Thyroid:
- Resume levothyroxine 25 mcg PO daily (held now while NPO; restart with NG or via crushed admin once NG placed).

Mental health:
- Esperanza Behavioral Health to be informed; Hopkins psych consult-liaison for inpatient continuity.
- PHQ-9 once stabilized.

Social work / care coordination:
- Insurance: emergency Medicaid active for admission. After stabilization, full Medicaid application + DSHS engagement with language support.
- Food insecurity post-discharge: anticipatory planning with food bank and SNAP application.
- Language: ensure consistent in-person Spanish interpretation during all rounding.
- Patient advocate identified; family meetings to be held every 48-72 hours.

Goals of care: I had a family meeting today (interpreter + patient + mother + aunt) discussing goals of care. Patient/family expressed clear desire for full-court-press treatment 'unless she stops being herself.' Code status: FULL CODE. DNR / DNI: NOT in effect. Will revisit GoC discussion as workup proceeds.

Anticipated length of stay: 10-14 days minimum given multi-system workup, nutritional rehabilitation requirements, and discharge planning complexity.

This admission H&P documented by Dr. Park (PGY-1), reviewed and modified by Dr. Castro (PGY-3), and electronically co-signed by Dr. Beckman (attending). Total documentation time including bedside interview with interpreter: 95 minutes.
attestation:
Electronically signed by:
Dr. David Park, MD (PGY-1, Internal Medicine) — primary author
Dr. Maria Castro, MD (PGY-3, Internal Medicine) — co-author and edits
Dr. Sarah Beckman, MD (Attending, Internal Medicine) — reviewing attending
Date: 3/15/2027
